result:
I would make the consequences of our actions on others truly, viscerally felt by the person causing them.

Not as a punishment, not as karma, but as an immediate, unavoidable feedback loop.

If you were to say something cruel to someone online, you would feel the sharp sting of their humiliation in your own chest. If a CEO made a decision that laid off a thousand people to boost a stock price, they would feel a thousand flickers of panic about paying rent, of a child's disappointment, of the dread of an uncertain future. If a politician voted to deny aid, they would feel the gnawing hunger or the shivering cold of those they condemned.

But it would also work for joy.

If you gave a genuine compliment, you would feel the warmth of it landing in the other person. If you created a piece of art that moved someone to tears, you would feel a wave of their catharsis. If you helped someone carry their groceries, you would feel their small, sincere moment of relief and gratitude.

It wouldn't be a voice of conscience telling you what's right or wrong. It would be direct, sensory data. To cause pain would be to experience a measure of that pain. To create joy would be to share in it.

Cruelty, in its purest form, would become an act of self-harm. Apathy would become impossible, because the suffering you chose to ignore would leave a cold spot in your own heart. Greed at the expense of others would have a bitter taste.

It wouldn't make everyone a saint. It wouldn't solve every disagreement. But it would change the fundamental calculus of human interaction. It would force us to acknowledge that we are not isolated islands, but a single, interconnected nervous system. The "other" would cease to be an abstraction.

It would be the end of casual cruelty. And that
